# Tempo Hierarchy

## Explains

Explains why different components of a system must operate at different clock speeds by design.

## The Concept

Strategy operates quarterly. Operations run daily. Incidents are measured in minutes. Code deploys hourly. Culture shifts over years. These different tempos are not a coordination failure — they are structural. Each layer of a system has a natural clock speed determined by the nature of the work, the cost of change, and the feedback loop latency at that level.

Systems that force everything to one speed produce pathological outcomes. If you run strategy at operational tempo, you get thrashing — direction changes daily, nothing compounds. If you run operations at strategic tempo, you get paralysis — quarterly reviews of issues that needed resolution hours ago. The tempos are different because the underlying physics are different. A building's foundation changes on a century timescale; its interior decoration changes on a decade timescale; its occupants change daily. Forcing uniform cadence on all three is absurd.

Governance must accommodate and explicitly design for multiple simultaneous tempos rather than assuming uniform cadence. This means different review cycles for different object types, different approval speeds for different risk levels, and different change propagation speeds for different layers of the system. A standing order (slow tempo, high stability) should not change at the same speed as a monitor configuration (fast tempo, high adaptability).

## Why This Matters for Governance

A governance system with a single cadence (e.g., quarterly review for everything) will be too slow for some things and too fast for others. JOS addresses this through review cadence metadata on each object and through the distinction between fast-changing instruments (SOPs, monitors) and slow-changing foundations (principles, standing orders). The tempo is encoded in the object's type, not imposed uniformly.

This also means that governance health metrics must be measured at the appropriate tempo. Checking strategic alignment daily produces noise. Checking operational health quarterly produces blindness. The monitoring tempo must match the governed tempo.

## Connects To

- JOS-C01 (Fractal Self-Similarity) — the same patterns appear at each tempo level, just at different speeds
- JOS-SO-003 (Review Cadence) — the standing order that operationalizes tempo-appropriate review
- Framework §14 (versioning/change propagation) — how changes at one tempo propagate to other layers

## Provenance

Foundry "Tempo Hierarchy" operations concept combined with FND-P21, reframed as a universal systems principle. Influenced by Stewart Brand's pace layering model (How Buildings Learn) and John Boyd's OODA loop tempo concepts.

### Framework Connection

Framework §15 (HAPPENS axis work types and cadence meta-component) maps tempo hierarchy into concrete governance by defining work types that operate at different speeds and a cadence meta-component that encodes each object's natural clock speed.
